content:
AI visibility is not SEO versus GEO versus AEO. It is the shift from ranking to recommendation — and the signal repair work brands now need.

10 min read
Just now

--

The fastest way to get an SEO person to ignore you is to tell them SEO is dead.
They’ve heard it for twenty years. Usually from someone trying to sell the next thing.
Most of what made SEO valuable still matters.
A site still has to be crawlable. The technical structure still has to hold. The content still needs to be useful, accessible and shaped around intent. Authority, internal linking, external proof and consistency still matter because machines still need signals before they can decide what deserves attention.
Strip those foundations out, and most AI visibility arguments collapse before they get interesting.
Google’s original search engine paper, published in 1998, described a system built to crawl and index the web efficiently while making heavy use of the structure inside hypertext. Links were not just paths between pages. They helped machines interpret the web at scale, follow relationships and decide which sources deserved attention.
So when someone says, “SEO already covers this,” they are not completely off track.
They’re just stopping before the new problem starts.
The history of SEO is really the history of search engines learning to understand more, while marketers are dragged away from whatever shortcut worked last year.
There was a time when keywords carried too much weight. Links became the next battleground. Content scale followed, and for a while, volume passed as usefulness. Technical health became harder to ignore. Intent changed the way good operators thought about pages. Entities, helpfulness, experience and proof kept pushing the work further away from tricks and closer to meaning.
Every time the system changed, the better operators adapted. The weaker operators kept selling the old trick until it stopped working.
One of the clearest shifts came with BERT. In 2019, Google said BERT helped Search better understand one in ten U.S. English searches at launch, especially longer and more conversational queries where small words changed the meaning. Google described it as one of the biggest leaps forward in the history of Search because it helped the system understand words in relation to the words around them, rather than treating a query as a bag of terms.
Good SEO people understood what that meant. The job was no longer “put the keyword on the page and build links until the graph bends.” It was becoming a discipline of making pages, brands and answers legible to systems trying to satisfy human intent.
“SEO is dead” is still a weak line. SEO has survived several supposed deaths by becoming broader each time.
The search result is no longer the only meaningful interface.
A person looking for a service used to move through a familiar pattern: type the query, scan the results, open a few tabs, compare pages, check reviews, ask around, then decide. The website had to earn the click and do the conversion work once the visitor arrived.
Now, a growing share of that comparison layer can occur before the click, around the click, or without the click.
Google’s AI Mode update explains query fan-out, where a question is broken into subtopics and multiple searches are issued at once on the user’s behalf. Deep Search takes the same technique further, with Google saying it can issue hundreds of searches, reason across separate pieces of information and produce a cited report in minutes — none of which resembles scrolling a ranked list of links.
Google’s own guidance for generative AI features also describes retrieval-augmented generation and query fan-out, where Search ranking systems retrieve relevant, up-to-date pages from the index, then review information from those pages to generate responses with supporting links. The same guide says foundational SEO best practices still matter because generative AI features in Google Search are rooted in core Search ranking and quality systems.
If SEO is dead, why does Google’s AI layer still depend on search infrastructure?
If SEO already covers everything, why does the interface now retrieve, assemble and summarise answers before the user has done the old comparison work?
Neither camp survives those questions intact.
Once the system starts expanding the query and assembling the answer, search behaves less like a directory and more like a synthesis layer.
A user asks a question. The system expands the query, pulls from multiple sources, weighs fragments against each other, generates a response and decides what deserves to be cited, linked, summarised or ignored.
The click may still happen, but it is no longer guaranteed to be the first meaningful interaction. A brand might appear as a cited source, as a passing sentence inside a generated answer, or not at all. A competitor might be named even when your page ranks well. The system may understand the category but miss the brand, recognise the brand but lack proof, or find proof that points to the wrong positioning.
AEO, GEO and traditional SEO are often presented as rival answers. Pick your fighter. Defend your acronym. Declare everything else fluff.
Good LinkedIn theatre. Terrible strategy.
AEO has a real place because answer systems need clear, extractable explanations. GEO matters because generative systems do not always source, rank or cite information in the same way as traditional organic search. Traditional SEO still matters because weak pages, broken crawl paths, thin content and poor technical foundations do not become irrelevant just because an AI summary now sits above the results.
LEO belongs in the same conversation for a different reason. Language models do not only need pages to crawl or answers to extract. They need clean brand language, consistent entity context and enough surrounding evidence to understand what a business does without flattening it into the wrong category.
The problem is treating any one of those layers as the whole job.
A 2026 study of Google AI Overviews found overall AI Overview activation of 13.7% across trending queries, rising to 64.7% for question-form queries. It also found that nearly 30% of AI Overview-cited domains did not appear in the co-displayed first-page organic results, suggesting source selection is not just traditional ranking with a new presentation.
Another empirical paper, comparing Google Search, Google AI Overviews and Gemini, found that AI Overviews appeared for 51.5% of representative real-user queries in its dataset. The same study found substantial differences between the sources retrieved by traditional search and generative search, with low overlap between systems.
That does not prove GEO is the answer.
It proves the answer is bigger than GEO.
Generative visibility depends on more than a citation. It is shaped by owned content, earned proof, structure, authority, language, recency, category fit and source confidence. Reduce that to GEO, and the work becomes citation chasing. Reduce it to AEO, and the brand problem gets mistaken for answer formatting. Fold it back into ordinary SEO, and you underplay how much evaluation now happens inside the synthesis layer.
A machine does not understand a brand because the brand says, “We are trusted.” It looks for corroboration.
That corroboration might come from the way the homepage describes the business, the way service pages explain the offer, the schema sitting underneath the page, the founder or entity footprint around the brand, third-party mentions, examples, reviews, testimonials, comparison content and signs that the business is still active.
If those signals point in different directions, the model has to reconcile the mess itself. If important pieces are missing, it fills the gaps with whatever else it can find. But when the public footprint is clear, current and consistent, the brand becomes easier to retrieve, easier to explain and easier to recommend.
When that public signal is weak, inconsistent or under-evidenced, the job is not just optimisation. It is repair.
That repair work sits awkwardly between SEO and brand strategy, which is probably why so much of the industry keeps trying to squeeze it back into a smaller box.
A ranking report can tell you where a page sits. It cannot always tell you whether an answer engine understands your business. A featured answer for one query does not prove a durable signal across engines, surfaces and tasks. And a generative citation, however valuable, is only one expression of a larger confidence problem.
A 2025 paper on Generative Engine Optimisation described AI search as moving from ranked lists toward synthesised, citation-backed answers. Its experiments found a strong bias toward earned media and third-party authoritative sources over brand-owned and social content. It also showed that AI search systems can differ from one another in freshness, domain diversity, language stability and sensitivity to phrasing.
The SEO-only view underestimates the role of external proof and machine confidence outside the owned website. The GEO-only view risks turning a whole visibility system into a game of “how do we get cited by AI?”
The work needs a wider container.
TEO exists because of that gap.
Tell Engine Optimisation is the framework for improving how a business is understood, trusted and recommended by AI-assisted systems.
It brings AEO, GEO and LEO into one system.
AEO is the answer layer. It helps a business become eligible for the questions, prompts and recommendation moments that matter.
GEO is the generation layer. It improves the context and source signals that shape how AI systems summarise, compare and cite a business.
LEO is the language layer. It makes the brand’s meaning clearer, more specific and easier for assistants to reuse without flattening it into the wrong category.
TEO sits above those layers. It is not competing with them. It gives them a shared job: improve recommendation confidence.
That is the missing piece in most of the current debate.
AEO can make answers clearer, but it does not automatically improve the wider context those answers are pulled from. GEO can improve generative visibility, but it can become citation chasing if the language still leaves room for misunderstanding. SEO can keep a site visible, but visibility alone does not guarantee the brand will be interpreted correctly or recommended with confidence.
Signal repair is the work that connects the system.
It asks whether the public footprint gives machines enough clear, consistent and corroborated evidence to understand what the business is, why it should be trusted and when it should be recommended.
That is why TEO is not SEO renamed, GEO repackaged or AEO with a bigger acronym.
It is the operating framework for the visibility problem brands are now facing: not just being found, but being interpreted correctly and recommended with confidence.
Google is right to say that, from Google Search’s perspective, optimising for generative AI search is still optimising for the search experience. Its guidance explicitly says AEO and GEO are terms used for visibility work in AI search experiences, but from Google Search’s perspective, optimising for generative AI search is still SEO. The same guide warns against unsupported shortcuts such as over-chunking content, unnecessary AI text files for Google Search and inauthentic mentions.
But brands do not live only inside Google’s definition of Search.
They live across Google Search, AI Overviews and AI Mode, but they also live inside ChatGPT search, Perplexity, Gemini and Copilot. They are shaped by review sites, comparison pages, directories, public profiles, third-party articles, social search, browser agents and whatever gets retrieved when a system tries to complete a task for a user.
OpenAI made that shift more obvious when it introduced ChatGPT search, describing it as a way to get fast, timely answers with links to relevant web sourc
